title: A learnability analysis on neuro-symbolic learning
abstract:
This paper presents a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the learnability of neuro-symbolic (NeSy) tasks within hybrid systems. 
  We characterize the learnability of NeSy tasks by their derived constraint satisfaction problems (DCSPs), demonstrating that a task is learnable if and only if its corresponding DCSP admits a unique solution. 
  Under mild assumptions, we establish the sample complexity for learnable tasks and show that, for general tasks, the asymptotic expected concept error is controlled by the degree of disagreement among DCSP solutions. 
  Our findings unify the characterization of learnability and the phenomenon of reasoning shortcuts, providing theoretical guarantees and actionable guidance for the principled design of NeSy systems.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of learnability in neurosymbolic AI, specifically of the well-known concept of distant supervision. The authors do this by modelling the task as a constraint satisfaction problem (CSP). They define the conditions for learnability in terms of this CSP and provide theoretical error bounds. They link this learnability to the well-established problem of reasoning shortcuts.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
- Strengths
    - The paper is generally well-written and should be well-understandable by members of the community.
    - The paper provides formal theoretical bounds on the concept error.
    - The paper contains experiments on multiple datasets that provide empirical evidence for the theory.
- Weaknesses
    - Some parts of the text are not clear to me (see questions below).
    - The experiments in the main text are all MNIST-based. While the appendices seem to have experiments on other datasets, I don’t find any information about their setup (somehow they seem to use the same “knowledge” as in MNIST, but I don’t understand how).
    - Some figures are very hard to read due to the small font (e.g. Figures 1, 2, 3).

QUESTIONS:
- In [1], the authors provide an algorithm for counting the number of reasoning shortcuts. They also use something similar to constraint solving (WMC). Is this not similar to the derived CSP you introduce? Can you elaborate on the difference?
- Line 45: You link your analysis with deterministic reasoning shortcuts. Is there also a link with probabilistic reasoning shortcuts (as defined by [2])?
- Equation 4: Shouldn’t there be a logarithm in front of p, like in Equation 5?
- Lines 141-145 of the related work are not really clear to me. What does “full knowledge of the underlying concept sequence distribution” mean exactly?	
- Line 178-184 and 191-194: This is not clear to me. Does the hypothesis space F refer to (1) the logic rules to compute the task from the concepts, or (2) the neural networks mapping input to concepts, or (3) both? To me, it seems either (1) or (3) is the case due to Proposition 3.2, but then lines 191-194 and the equation below line 194 point to (2). If (1), then why do these lines refer to for instance neural networks? If (2) or (3), then why does f map input to concepts in the equation below line 194? 
- As a consequence of the above point, I’m not sure what the “restricted hypothesis space” exactly refers to (as I’m not sure what the “hypothesis space” exactly refers to).
- Line 202: Is there a reason why the different variables V_i have the same domain Z?
- Lines 265-270: I would work this example more out. I don’t immediately see why, when k=8, there are 4 solutions and d/L=0.4. What are the exact values for d and L, and maybe you can briefly give some examples of solutions.
- In Figure 3, can you give some intuition on why the reasoning accuracy is bad for the aggregation?
- What is the setup for the CIFAR-10 and SVHN datasets (Appendix line 756)?
- Can you elaborate on what you state in lines 769-770: “optimization becomes significantly more challenging due to high conflicts among valid DCSP solutions”. Why is reasoning accuracy so low when there are shortcuts (e.g. Figure 9 for k=2)?

I would be happy to increase my score if the above remarks are clarified.

[1] A Neuro-Symbolic Benchmark Suite for Concept Quality and Reasoning Shortcuts. Bortolotti et al.

[2] Not All Neuro-Symbolic Concepts Are Created Equal: Analysis and Mitigation of Reasoning Shortcuts. Marconato et al.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper provides a formal theoretical analysis of the learnability of neuro-symbolic (NeSy) tasks, particularly within hybrid systems combining neural learning and symbolic reasoning. It introduces the concept of mapping a NeSy task to a Derived Constraint Satisfaction Problem (DCSP) and shows that a NeSy task is learnable if and only if the associated DCSP admits a unique solution (i.e., zero solution disagreement). The authors derive sample complexity bounds, an asymptotic error bound related to solution disagreement, and discuss how unlearnable tasks may become learnable through aggregation. Empirical results using tasks like addition and modular addition support the theoretical claims.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- Novel Theoretical Framework: The use of DCSPs as a lens for understanding learnability is novel, precise, and potentially impactful for both theory and practice in NeSy.
- Connection to Reasoning Shortcuts: It links its theoretical results to practical phenomena like reasoning shortcuts, which is a major known challenge in NeSy.
- Empirical Validation The experiments demonstrate how solution disagreement affects learnability in practice, providing convincing evidence for the theory.

Weaknesses:
- Experiments: While the experiments are relevant, they are mostly based on toy tasks (digit operations) and do not yet extend to more realistic or open-ended NeSy benchmarks.
- Scalability Concerns: For large-scale tasks or richer symbolic domains, solving DCSPs can be intractable.

QUESTIONS:
- Your framework requires formulating the DCSP and computing its solution set and the disagreement measure $d$ (Definition 3.3). For realistic neuro-symbolic tasks with large or continuous concept spaces, how tractable is it to exactly compute or approximate all DCSP solutions? If exact enumeration is infeasible, what approximate methods or heuristics would you recommend to estimate disagreement in practice?

- Your Corollary 3.7 proposes that aggregating multiple unlearnable tasks can reduce solution disagreement and yield learnability. s there an algorithmic selection method, or must this be done manually via domain knowledge?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors propose a theoretical framework to characterise the 
learnability within hybrid NeSy systems with respect to the 
sample complexity asymptotically bounding the concept error.  

The theoretical analysis considers the derived constraint 
satisfaction problem (DCSP) for a NeSy learning task and 
proposes as an indicator of its ambiguity *solution disagreement*, 
measuring the degree of disagreement among DCSP solutions

The main results of the paper are Theorem 3.5 and 3.6 showing 
respectively that a task is learnable iff the associated DCSP has 
a unique solution, and the asymptotic expected concept error is 
controlled by solution disagreement

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
# Soundness
The paper is technically sound, the main results precisely fromulated
and the proofs presented with the appropriate rigor. The experimental
section is correctly focused on assessing the content of the main 
theoretical results.  

# Presentation
The article is well-written and its presentation is satisfactory.
The content of Sections 3.5 and 4 could benefit from further additions
(please refer to 'Other Comments') 

# Contribution
The main contribution of the article is the DCSP framework allowing 
to define learnable and unlearnable NeSy tasks and bounding the 
expected concept risk of an arbitrary hypothesis. The experimental
evaluation briefly validates the findings.

# Strengths
The DCSP framework addresses a relevant problem, it is quite 
straighforward in its construction and application and succeeds 
in giving a detailed perspective for concept error analysis.
It is applicable both for Probabilistic Neuro-Symbolic and 
Abductive Learning. The analysis considers ERM algorithm that 
is in line with a large part of the literature on this topic.
Lastly, the perspective of DCSPs combination for unlearnable 
tasks gives interesting insights. 

# Weaknesses 
The main issues lie in the assumptions this work is making.
First, the *restricted hypothesis space* assumption. 
Second, some other assumptions limiting the applicability of 
the framework are implicit. In particular, Assumption 3.4. 
entails that all the possible combinations of concepts yielding
some final label y must have non-zero probability of occurring.
However, in many cases the support of the data is not full
(e.g. in MNIST-EvenOdd for y = 9 the concept combination 
<3,6> never occurs as only pairs of odd/even digits are observed).
Please also see the 'Other Comments' Section of the review.


# Other Comments
Please consider the additional comments below.

The article would benefit from an extention of Section 3.5.,
for example the authors could give further insights on the 
*conditions* that are generally required in order for the 
combination of unlearnable tasks to become learnable.

In Definition 3.1., $\mathcal{A}$ should be expressively 
linked to the learned hypothesis $\hat{f}$ 
(e.g., $\mathcal{A} = \hat{f}$).

Proposition 3.2. should be more clear, as defining the hypothesis space
as *sufficiently complex* is not formal enough. I suggest making the 
shattering property explicit in the statement and not in brackets.
Here the authors could consider to insert an inline definition for 
shattering right before the Proposition and explain shortly it is
related to the complexity of the space.
 
The experimental evaluation is restricted to cases where the 
background knowledge are simple mathematical operations. 
It would be interesting to see some examples with other types
of (more complex) constraints.  

# Overall Evaluation
The theoretical framework proposed by the article is relevant 
and sound. The Presentation is clear and the contributions 
allow to characterise prominent learnability problems.
However the assumptions are quite limiting and the experimental
evaluation could be extended with at least another more complex
case. In its current state I consider this article to be 
marginally below the acceptance threshold.

QUESTIONS:
See the Strengths and Weaknesses section.

LIMITATIONS:
The paper clearly discusses the limitations of the framework,
namely the exclusive focus on hybrid NeSy models and the 
restricted hypothesis space. Among the two the latter is 
argubly the one limiting the most the applicability of the 
framework proposed.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors propose a well-structured and grounded theoretical analysis of the learnability of neuro-symbolic (NeSy) tasks. Such a theoretical analysis relies on mapping NeSy tasks into constraints satisfiability problems, showcasing that a task is learnable if and only if its corresponding DCSP admits a unique solution. This finding represents a very relevant result of this paper, which will probably become helpful for the NeSy community. Moreover, the authors provide a brief experimental evaluation to empirically back their theoretical findings, proving a solid paper. Overall, the paper is also relatively easy to read and follow, and its main limitations are linked to a shallow discussion of some relevant aspects, a somewhat limited experimental evaluation, and the missing source code to reproduce the experimental results. Therefore, the paper is of good quality while still requiring quite some polishing from a presentation and experimental perspective.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- The paper tackles a relevant issue of NeSy models.
- The paper showcases relevant and important findings about some NeSy models, which will probably become helpful for the NeSy community.
- The theoretical analysis proposed seems solid and well-grounded.
- The experimental evaluation backs the theoretical findings identified by the authors.

Weaknesses:
- A few aspects should be discussed more in detail.
- Experimental evaluation could be made more extensive.
- The paper focuses only on probabilistic neuro-symbolic and abductive learning methods while not considering other NeSy models, such as symbolic knowledge injection (SKI) models [1].
- Missing source code.

[1]. Ciatto, Giovanni, et al. "Symbolic knowledge extraction and injection with sub-symbolic predictors: A systematic literature review." ACM Computing Surveys 56.6 (2024): 1-35.

QUESTIONS:
- I know that the authors do not have enough space to provide detailed proof of the theorems and lemmas in the main body of the paper. However, the authors should also consider giving an intuition of the theorems' proofs in the main body of the paper to ease its understandability. For example, it would be great to have an intuition of why Theorem 2.1 holds true at the end of section 2.2. Could the authors elaborate on this point?
- What is L in the definition of the DCSP solution disagreement d in Section 3.3? L does not seem to be used in the paper before the equation in which d is defined and is not described after.
- Can the authors provide more details about the definition of the Union() operation in the solutions set? The authors mention that the Union captures the common assignments among the solutions. However, it is not completely clear what that means. Is it the way to identify solutions that map different concepts to the same label?
- Can the authors provide more details on the feasibility of Assumption 3.4 in a real-world scenario? This assumption is presented very quickly and without much justification, thus making it complex for the reader to understand if it makes sense or not. I understand that this is a mild assumption, but I do not understand in which cases it does or does not hold. It seems that it should not hold only in the case of a continuous concept space; is this correct?
- The authors skim through the results of Theorem 3.5 too quickly if considering the relevance of the provided Theorem. Could the authors provide a more in-depth discussion of the findings connected with Theorem 3.5? From my understanding, it seems that whenever the DCSP solution is not unique (i.e., d > 0), the NeSy task is not learnable, and thus, there will always be a concept risk greater than 0. This, in turn, would mean that reasoning shortcuts are unavoidable whenever the DCSP solution is not unique. Moreover, my understanding is that in general real-world scenarios DCSP solutions are not unique with a high probability. Thus, the findings suggest that in real-world scenarios, reasoning shortcuts and other issues related to concept risks are unavoidable. Is this correct?
- From Table 1, it seems that the addition and multiplication tasks are perfectly learnable tasks. However, these tasks can still be affected by reasoning shortcuts. For example, whenever a model learns to swap the representation of the two digits being summed up, the addition or multiplication will result in the proper label but the wrong concepts. Therefore, it is not clear what the connection between learnability and reasoning shortcuts is. Could the authors clarify this distinction and discuss it more in-depth? I believe that this is an important aspect to clarify the contributions and impact of the paper better.
- Why did the authors consider focusing only on digit summation and multiplication tasks? Several other NeSy tasks exist that are much more relevant to real-world scenarios. I would suggest the authors extend their experimental evaluation to other datasets and tasks.
The paper focuses only on probabilistic neuro-symbolic and abductive learning methods while not considering other available NeSy models, such as symbolic knowledge injection (SKI) models [1]. I would suggest the authors add a brief discussion about this, as well as some references to other NeSy models like SKI [2,3,4]. Could the authors add such a discussion?

[1]. Ciatto, Giovanni, et al. "Symbolic knowledge extraction and injection with sub-symbolic predictors: A systematic literature review." ACM Computing Surveys 56.6 (2024): 1-35.

[2]. Agiollo, Andrea, et al. "Symbolic knowledge injection meets intelligent agents: QoS metrics and experiments." Autonomous Agents and Multi-Agent Systems 37.2 (2023): 27.

[3]. Von Rueden, Laura, et al. "Informed machine learning–a taxonomy and survey of integrating prior knowledge into learning systems." IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering 35.1 (2021): 614-633.

[4]. Magnini, Matteo, Giovanni Ciatto, and Andrea Omicini. "On the design of PSyKI: a platform for symbolic knowledge injection into sub-symbolic predictors." International Workshop on Explainable, Transparent Autonomous Agents and Multi-Agent Systems. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2022.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (spotlight)

COMMENT:
Solid technical paper of interest to NeSy practitioners and researchers alike. All reviewers agree that its strengths outweight its weaknesses, which aren't many. The author-reviewer discussion was very polite and productive. I recommend the authors integrate all the promised changes (i.e., improving clarity of the text and figures where requested; including the BDD-OIA experiment) into the revision.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
